section_1___patient_information.ordering_physician_practice: Dr. Alan K. Rosen, Strong Memorial Hospital Oncology

section_3___financial_information.additional_income_please_specify:
Daniel occasionally earns small amounts from freelance graphic design work (approximately $150–$250 per month, averaging around $2,000 per year before taxes). This income is inconsistent and not guaranteed, depending on when former clients reach out for small logo updates or brochure layouts. Caroline does not have any additional earned income beyond what is listed, as she has been on a reduced work schedule due to ongoing cancer treatment, which has limited her ability to take on extra hours or side work.

They also receive about $600 per year in small cash gifts from Caroline’s parents around birthdays and holidays, which they generally use to offset prescription copays or unexpected medical-related expenses such as parking at the hospital, over-the-counter medications, and nutritional supplements recommended by her care team. These gifts are not regular monthly support but tend to come in two or three lump sums spread across the year.

No rental income, investment dividends, or child support/alimony is received. They do not have any significant savings generating interest; their checking and savings accounts typically maintain low balances (under $1,000 combined) because most of their income is used to cover rent, utilities, food, transportation, and medical bills. They also do not receive any veterans’ benefits, disability payments, or pension income at this time.
section_3___financial_information.other_health_care_expenses_amounts_and_h:
Caroline and her family have several ongoing health care–related expenses that significantly affect their monthly budget:

1. Oncology and specialist copays: Caroline typically has 2–3 oncology or specialist visits per month. Each visit has a copay of $45–$60 depending on whether it is considered a specialist or follow-up visit. On average, this totals about $130–$160 per month. These visits are mandatory as part of her ongoing cancer monitoring and treatment planning.

2. Prescription medications: Caroline takes several medications related to her cancer treatment and side-effect management (including anti-nausea medication, pain management, and a proton pump inhibitor for reflux). Even with insurance, her out-of-pocket cost averages about $220–$260 per month. Some months are higher when a medication is changed or dosage is increased. In addition, her husband Daniel takes a generic blood pressure medication that costs them around $15 per month after insurance.

3. Imaging and lab work: Every few months, Caroline has CT scans, MRIs, and frequent blood work. While some of these are mostly covered by insurance, she is often billed afterward for coinsurance and deductibles. On average, over the last year, this has translated into about $75–$100 per month when averaged out (for example, a $300 bill every three to four months). These bills often arrive weeks after the services, making budgeting difficult.

4. Counseling and mental health support: Caroline has been seeing a therapist who specializes in oncology-related anxiety and depression. The counselor is in-network but still requires a $30 copay per session. Caroline goes twice per month when possible, totaling about $60 per month. She considers this essential to coping with the emotional burden of her diagnosis and treatment.

5. Over-the-counter and supportive care items: Each month, they spend approximately $40–$60 on over-the-counter medications and supportive products related to treatment side effects and general health maintenance. This includes things like stool softeners, gentle skin creams for radiation-affected skin, electrolyte drinks, nutritional shakes when Caroline’s appetite is low, and special mouth rinses to help with mouth sores.

6. Transportation and parking for medical visits: They own one older car and use it primarily to get to and from the hospital and clinic visits. Gas and parking for medical appointments cost an additional $50–$70 per month on average. Parking at the hospital is usually $6–$10 per visit, and with multiple appointments some weeks, this adds up quickly. When their car has minor issues, repair costs further strain their budget, though those are not strictly medical expenses, they are directly related to being able to get to treatment.

Altogether, these health care–related expenses typically total between $500 and $650 per month, which is a substantial portion of their take-home pay. Because their income is modest and relatively fixed, any unexpected bill (such as an out-of-network lab charge or a new imaging copay) often forces them to delay other payments, rely on credit cards, or ask family for short-term help. This ongoing financial pressure is one of the main reasons they are applying for financial assistance through this program.
